title: Why I Quit My Dev Job Before AI Could Take It | Better Stack Podcast Ep. 16
summary: Elliot, a developer and content creator, discusses his transition away from traditional development work and his views on AI's role in software engineering. He shares his practical workflow with AI tools (using Claude Code and Codex for stubs and interfaces), his belief that LLMs are hitting diminishing returns, and his prediction of declining demand for coding tutorials.
writeup: A podcast interview with Elliot, creator of the Dreams of Code and Dreams of Autonomy YouTube channels, covering his path from C++ and Vim in finance to terminal-centric development and CLI tool content. The conversation traces how AI coding agents changed his workflow and stack choices, how Claude Code rewrote his Go-plus-HTMX course platform into Next.js, and the real economics of developer YouTube where AdSense yields only a couple thousand dollars a month and course revenue alone is not enough to sustain full-time work. The through-line is a practitioner's honest account of the gap between building things and the marketing required to make them pay.
key_points: Elliot credits a university compiler module, which required SSH-into-Unix-box Vim work in C++, as the origin of his Neovim practice, reinforced by his first finance job under identical constraints, making it a career-long tool rather than an aesthetic choice.

Neovim without AI autocomplete is now his deliberate reading and navigation environment: he uses Claude Code and Codex in the terminal for generation, keeping Neovim purely for code comprehension and targeted edits, a split that the hosts noted is pulling even non-Vim users away from tools like Cursor that bundle too much AI UI into the editor.

Zoxide, which Elliot surfaced through forum lurking and GitHub star searches, illustrates his tool-discovery method: it solves a concrete pain (painful directory navigation), and once adopted it becomes impossible to go back to plain cd.

Claude Code rewrote Elliot's Go-plus-HTMX course platform into Next.js early in the agent wave, and he found it capable enough to complete that migration, which is how he ended up on Next.js rather than finishing the Alpine Ajax path he had been evaluating.

HTMX with Go and the Templ package works for server-rendered hypermedia sites but hits a wall when client-side interactivity grows: vanilla JavaScript organization without a framework becomes harder than the framework itself, a finding Elliot reached while nearly reaching for jQuery.

YouTube AdSense for a developer channel at Elliot's scale yields roughly a couple thousand dollars a month, which he describes as not livable in the US; his course on Go CLI development made decent money but not enough for full-time work, and he attributes the shortfall mostly to minimal marketing effort rather than lack of demand.
